title: Either...Or: How One Word Changes Everything
subtitle: Staub & Clifton 2006 and the power of syntactic prediction

children.children: Read this word:
Either...

Feel that? Something just happened in your brain. You're not done reading. You're WAITING. Specifically, you're waiting for 'or.' You can't help it. The moment you read 'either,' your parser opened a slot — a structural expectation that MUST be filled. 'Either' without 'or' is like an opening parenthesis without a close. Your brain won't rest until the pattern completes.

This is SYNTACTIC PREDICTION. Your parser doesn't just analyze what it's already seen — it anticipates what's coming. And this is different from simply guessing the next word. You're not predicting any particular word after 'either.' You're predicting a STRUCTURE: that somewhere downstream, an 'or' will appear, creating a coordination.

props.title: Feel the Pull
props.prompt: Read each sentence fragment. Notice how 'either' creates a structural expectation — a pull toward 'or' — that you can feel in real time.

scenarios.input: Either the actress or the director will attend.
scenarios.reveal: Comfortable, right? 'Either' opened a slot, 'or' filled it. Pattern complete. Your brain relaxes.

scenarios.input: Either the dog ran across the yard...
scenarios.reveal: You're waiting. WHERE IS THE 'OR'? Your brain has opened a structural slot and it's still open. You can feel the incompleteness — like a held breath. This is syntactic prediction at work.

scenarios.input: Either the professor gave a lecture...
scenarios.reveal: Still waiting. Still uncomfortable. Your parser KNOWS a coordination must come. It's not guessing what specific word comes next — it's holding open an architectural blueprint that demands an 'or' branch. This is structural prediction, not lexical prediction.

scenarios.input: The dog ran across the yard.
scenarios.reveal: No tension at all. Without 'either,' there's no open slot. Your parser processes this reactively, word by word, with no forward-looking structural expectation. The sentence could end anywhere and you'd be fine.

children.children: This distinction matters: LEXICAL prediction means guessing the next specific word ('The doctor grabbed the...scalpel?'). STRUCTURAL prediction means knowing that a syntactic pattern must complete, without knowing the specific words that will fill it. 'Either' triggers structural prediction. Your brain doesn't know what comes after 'or' — but it knows 'or' MUST come.

props.analogy: Imagine driving on a highway and seeing a sign that says 'FORK AHEAD.' You don't know whether you'll go left or right. You don't know what's on either branch. But you KNOW the road will split. That's structural prediction. Lexical prediction would be seeing a sign that says 'GAS STATION 1 MILE' — you know exactly what's coming. 'Either' is the fork-ahead sign.
props.insight: 'Either' doesn't tell you what words are coming. It tells you what SHAPE the sentence will take. The road will fork. That's the prediction.

children.children: In 2006, Adrian Staub and Charles Clifton Jr. designed an elegant experiment to test whether 'either' actually helps readers process 'or' faster. The logic was simple: if 'either' lets you predict 'or,' then when 'or' finally arrives, it should be EASIER to process — like a package you were already expecting.
They compared sentences WITH and WITHOUT 'either':
WITH 'either' (predicted 'or'):
'Either the actress or the director will be at the premiere.'
WITHOUT 'either' (unpredicted 'or'):
'The actress or the director will be at the premiere.'
The critical measurement: reading time at the word 'or.' If 'either' helps, readers should be FASTER at 'or' when they've already seen 'either.'
And they were. Reading times at 'or' were significantly faster when 'either' was present. The road sign worked — readers who had seen the 'FORK AHEAD' sign navigated the fork more smoothly.
But here's where it gets interesting. Staub and Clifton tested TWO types of coordination, and the facilitation only showed up for one of them.
NP-coordination (joining two noun phrases):
'Either the actress or the director will attend.' — Two nouns joined by 'or.'
S-coordination (joining two clauses):
'Either the actress rehearsed her lines or the director chose the costumes.' — Two full clauses joined by 'or.'
The 'either' advantage in reading times? It only appeared for S-COORDINATION. For NP-coordination, 'either' didn't speed things up in the same way.
Why? Because S-coordination creates a GARDEN-PATH opportunity that 'either' prevents. And NP-coordination doesn't.

props.title: The Ambiguity That 'Either' Prevents
props.prompt: Watch what happens when your parser encounters 'or' in an S-coordination sentence WITHOUT 'either.' The ambiguity is real — and painful.

scenarios.reveal: STOP. Your parser is in trouble. 'Or' could mean two things: (1) 'The actress [rehearsed her lines] or [did something else]' — the actress did one thing OR another (S-coordination). (2) 'The actress rehearsed [her lines or her monologue]' — she rehearsed one thing OR another (NP-coordination of objects). Without 'either,' you don't know which structure you're in until MORE words arrive. Your parser has to GUESS — and it might guess wrong.

scenarios.input: 'EITHER the actress rehearsed her lines or...'
scenarios.reveal: No ambiguity! 'Either' at the start of the sentence tells you this is S-coordination — two CLAUSES are being coordinated, not two noun phrases. When 'or' arrives, you already know the structure. No garden path. No reparse. The fork was signposted.

With NP-coordination, there's NO ambiguity whether or not 'either' is present. 'The actress or the director' can only be two nouns joined by 'or.' There's no alternative parse to stumble into. So 'either' has nothing to prevent — no garden path to avoid. The road sign says 'FORK AHEAD' on a road that was already clearly forking.

children.children: Let's walk through the garden path in slow motion. This is the heart of Staub and Clifton's finding.
WITHOUT 'either': 'The actress rehearsed her lines or the director chose the costumes.'

Your parser reads 'The actress rehearsed her lines' and builds a perfectly good simple sentence. Subject, verb, object. Done. Then 'or' arrives and everything breaks. 'Or the director' — wait, is this a NEW clause? Then 'her lines' wasn't the end of the sentence, it was the end of the FIRST clause. Your parser has to REPARSE. The simple-sentence analysis you built? Tear it down. Rebuild as a coordination of two clauses.
WITH 'either': 'Either the actress rehearsed her lines or the director chose the costumes.'

Now your parser reads 'Either' and immediately knows: a coordination is coming. Two alternatives. When you read 'The actress rehearsed her lines,' you DON'T commit to a simple-sentence analysis. You KNOW this is just the first branch. When 'or' arrives, it's expected. No reparse. No broken structure. You just smoothly transition to the second branch.

props.analogy: You're driving at 60 mph on a country road. Scenario A: No sign. The road suddenly forks. You slam the brakes, swerve, maybe take the wrong branch and have to U-turn. That's reading S-coordination without 'either.' Scenario B: A sign says 'FORK AHEAD — 500 ft.' You slow down, prepare, and when the fork comes, you smoothly take your branch. That's reading S-coordination WITH 'either.' The road is identical in both cases. The only difference is whether you had warning.
props.insight: 'Either' doesn't change the sentence's structure. It changes your PREPARATION for that structure. The fork is the same fork. But with warning, you navigate it without crashing.

props.title: Why NP-Coordination Doesn't Show This Effect

children.children: Consider: 'The actress or the director will attend.'

When you read 'The actress,' you're building a noun phrase. When 'or' arrives, you simply extend it: 'The actress or the director' — still a noun phrase, just a compound one. There's no competing analysis. At no point could you have been garden-pathed into a wrong structure. 'Or' joins two nouns, and that was the only option.

Adding 'either' — 'Either the actress or the director' — still gives you prediction (you expect 'or'), and it may even lower LEXICAL surprisal at 'or.' But there's no garden path to prevent. The structural prediction doesn't buy you anything extra because the structure was never ambiguous.
This is the key insight: 'either' helps most when it PREVENTS A MISPARSE, not just when it predicts a word. The benefit is about structural disambiguation, not mere prediction.

If 'either' helps human readers by enabling structural prediction, does it help a neural language model the same way? We tested this in GPT-2, using 15 S-coordination pairs and 8 NP-coordination pairs — each pair consisting of one sentence with 'either' and one without.
We measured two things at the word 'or':
SURPRISAL: How unexpected is 'or'? Lower surprisal = 'or' was more expected. This captures LEXICAL prediction — did the model see 'or' coming?

L11 CONSENSUS: How much do Layer 11 neurons agree about the structural analysis? Higher consensus = the model has a confident, unified parse. This captures STRUCTURAL prediction — does the model know what kind of structure it's in?
The results were striking — and they split along exactly the lines Staub and Clifton's theory predicts.

props.title: The GPT-2 Results
props.prompt: Explore what happened when GPT-2 processed sentences with and without 'either.' The pattern mirrors human reading times — but reveals something deeper.

scenarios.reveal: 'Either' LOWERED surprisal at 'or' (d = 0.69, p = .019). The model saw 'or' coming when 'either' was present. This is lexical prediction — 'either' made 'or' more expected as a specific word. The road sign helped the model anticipate the fork.

'Either' RAISED L11 consensus at 'or' (d = −0.93, p = .003). With 'either,' the model's structural neurons were more unified — they agreed on what kind of structure they were processing. Without 'either,' consensus dropped at 'or' as the model scrambled to reparse. This is structural prediction — the model had a confident parse BEFORE 'or' arrived.

'Either' LOWERED surprisal at 'or' for NP-coordination too (p < .001). The lexical prediction effect works for both types — 'either' always makes 'or' more expected as a word. The road sign always tells you a fork is coming, regardless of road type.

'Either' had NO EFFECT on L11 consensus for NP-coordination (p = .80). The structural prediction effect is ABSENT. Why? Because NP-coordination has no structural ambiguity — there's no garden path to prevent, no competing parse to resolve. L11 consensus was already fine without 'either.' The road sign doesn't help on a road that was never confusing.

This is the key: surprisal and L11 consensus DISSOCIATE. Surprisal drops for both coordination types (lexical prediction). Consensus rises only for S-coordination (structural prediction). This means L11 consensus tracks something DIFFERENT from word expectation — it tracks structural prediction error. It's not asking 'did I expect this word?' but 'do I know what kind of structure I'm in?'

children.children: KEY INSIGHT: L11 tracks PREDICTION ERROR at the structural level, not just lexical surprisal. The 'either' effect on consensus appears at the prediction point ('or') — the moment where the structural commitment must be made — not at the point where the structure gets complex (the second verb). This means L11 is monitoring structural confidence in real time, flagging moments where the parser's predictions are confirmed or violated.

props.title: What This Tells Us About Parsing

children.children: The either/or story reveals something profound about how parsers — both human and artificial — actually work. They are PREDICTIVE, not just reactive. They don't wait for structure to arrive and then analyze it. They USE forward-looking information to prepare for structure before it arrives.

'Either' doesn't make a sentence simpler. The sentence 'Either the actress rehearsed her lines or the director chose the costumes' is just as complex as 'The actress rehearsed her lines or the director chose the costumes.' Same words (minus one). Same structure. Same meaning. The only difference is PREDICTABILITY. 'Either' shifts the processing difficulty from the point of structural commitment ('or') to the point of the cue ('either') — spreading the work over time instead of concentrating it at the fork.

This connects directly to Levy's surprisal theory: processing difficulty at any word is proportional to how much that word changes the probability distribution over possible structures. Without 'either,' the word 'or' in an S-coordination sentence causes a massive shift — suddenly, a simple sentence becomes a coordination, and you have to reparse. With 'either,' the probability mass was ALREADY shifted toward the coordination analysis. When 'or' arrives, it confirms what you expected. Low surprisal. Low processing cost. Smooth sailing.

props.analogy: Remember the PPP — the Preliminary Phrase Packager from Frazier & Fodor's Sausage Machine? The PPP can USE 'either' to prepare the right package shape. It sees 'either' and knows: I'm making a TWO-BRANCH package. Each branch is a clause. When 'or' arrives, the PPP has already cut the casing to the right size. Without 'either,' the PPP makes a SINGLE-CLAUSE package. Then 'or' arrives and the package is wrong. The SSS — the Sentence Structure Supervisor — has to intervene, rip open the package, and repackage everything as a coordination. That's expensive.
props.insight: 'Either' is advance instructions for the sausage machine. It tells the PPP what shape of sausage to make BEFORE the meat arrives.

children.children: The road sign analogy has held all the way through — from human reading times, to structural ambiguity, to GPT-2's neural responses. 'Either' is a sign that says 'FORK AHEAD.' It doesn't change the road. It changes how you DRIVE on it. And that, it turns out, makes all the difference.

questions.question: What kind of prediction does 'either' trigger?
options: Lexical prediction — you guess the next specific word
Structural prediction — you know a syntactic pattern (coordination with 'or') must complete
Semantic prediction — you guess the meaning of the sentence
Phonological prediction — you anticipate the sound of the next word
questions.explanation: 'Either' triggers STRUCTURAL prediction — you know that an 'or' coordination must follow, but you don't know what specific words will fill in each branch. It's like seeing a 'FORK AHEAD' road sign: you know the road will split, but you don't know what's on each branch.

questions.question: Why did Staub & Clifton find that 'either' helped reading times for S-coordination but NOT NP-coordination?
options: S-coordination sentences are longer
S-coordination creates a garden-path ambiguity that 'either' prevents, while NP-coordination has no such ambiguity
NP-coordination is too simple for 'either' to matter
Readers skip 'either' in NP-coordination sentences
questions.explanation: In S-coordination, without 'either,' the parser initially builds a simple-sentence analysis and then has to REPARSE at 'or.' 'Either' prevents this garden path by signaling coordination in advance. In NP-coordination ('The actress or the director'), there's no competing simple-sentence analysis — 'or' joins two nouns unambiguously. So 'either' has no garden path to prevent.

questions.question: In our GPT-2 experiment, what did the DISSOCIATION between surprisal and L11 consensus reveal?
options: GPT-2 can't handle coordination
Surprisal and consensus measure the same thing
L11 consensus tracks structural prediction error, not just lexical expectation — it responds to structural ambiguity, not word frequency
The experiment failed for NP-coordination
questions.explanation: Surprisal dropped for BOTH coordination types (lexical prediction), but L11 consensus rose only for S-coordination (structural prediction). This dissociation shows that L11 consensus tracks something different from word-level expectation — it monitors whether the model has a confident structural analysis, flagging moments of structural uncertainty.

questions.question: According to Levy's surprisal theory, WHY does 'either' reduce processing difficulty at 'or'?
options: It makes 'or' a more frequent word
It shifts probability mass toward the coordination analysis BEFORE 'or' arrives, so 'or' causes less re-ranking
It slows down reading so the brain has more time
It makes the sentence shorter
questions.explanation: Surprisal theory says processing difficulty is proportional to how much a word changes the probability distribution over possible structures. Without 'either,' 'or' causes a massive shift from simple-sentence to coordination. With 'either,' the coordination analysis already has high probability — so 'or' merely confirms what was expected, causing minimal re-ranking and minimal processing cost.

questions.question: How does the 'either' effect connect to the Sausage Machine model?
options: It proves the Sausage Machine is wrong
'Either' lets the PPP prepare the correct two-branch package shape before 'or' arrives, avoiding costly repackaging by the SSS
'Either' only affects the SSS, not the PPP
The Sausage Machine can't handle coordination
questions.explanation: 'Either' gives the PPP advance instructions: this sentence is a coordination — make a two-branch package. Without 'either,' the PPP makes a single-clause package, and when 'or' arrives, the SSS has to rip it open and repackage. 'Either' is like advance sausage-making instructions — tell the machine what shape to make BEFORE the meat arrives.

children.children: • Staub, A. & Clifton, C. Jr. (2006). 'Syntactic prediction in language comprehension: Evidence from either...or.' Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 32(2), 425–436.
• Levy, R. (2008). 'Expectation-based syntactic comprehension.' Cognition, 106(3), 1126–1177.
• Frazier, L. & Fodor, J.D. (1978). 'The Sausage Machine: A New Two-Stage Parsing Model.' Cognition, 6, 291–325.
